Do an adversarial review of https://entire.io/gh/entireio/entire-graph/trails/8 - you can access the trail via the Entire CLI.

I’ll load the trail through Entire, reconstruct its commits and intent, then inspect the resulting diff for correctness, regressions, and contract violations. I’ll report only actionable findings, with precise file/line references.

The local checkout is already on the trail’s branch (`fix/corpus-parse-errors`), but the trail metadata endpoint reports that the CLI is not authenticated. I’ll continue from the checked-out branch and its upstream diff while checking whether the local Entire session has another usable trail-access path.

The checkpoint context is available locally even though the hosted trail view requires login. It confirms this is a corpus-driven parser-mask patch: SQL, TS/TSX, and Bash syntax are rewritten position-preservingly to compensate for grammar gaps. I’m now testing the masks against adversarial inputs, especially comments/strings, token boundaries, and cases where byte-preserving rewrites can silently alter extracted symbols.

The adversarial probes have reproduced two concrete regressions and one coverage hole: a valid TSX runtime import is turned into a syntax error, SQL text containing a comma-list `DROP` can corrupt surrounding DDL, and valid multi-name `DROP TYPE`/`DROP MATERIALIZED VIEW` statements remain unhandled. I’m cleaning up the probes and running the repository’s normal verification so the review distinguishes branch failures from unrelated test failures.

content.text: I’ll implement the fixes at the masking layer and add regression tests for each reproduced case: nested runtime `typeof import(...)`, SQL strings containing DDL text, and additional PostgreSQL multi-name `DROP` variants. Then I’ll run the race suite, vet, and build.

The code and regression tests are in place. A direct `go test` hit a local toolchain mismatch (`go1.26.1` driver against `go1.24.13` standard-library artifacts), so I’m switching verification to the repository’s mise-pinned Go 1.24 environment rather than treating that infrastructure error as a code failure.

All seven targeted regressions now pass, including the original corpus cases. The fixes are deliberately narrow: quoted module-specifier matching for TypeScript type queries, a position-preserving SQL literal search view, and the complete set of simple PostgreSQL comma-list DROP object kinds. I’m reviewing the diff for offset stability before the full suite.

content.text: Race testing is still running in the large `internal/sem` package; all completed packages are green so far. The only output is the repository’s existing tree-sitter/Apple linker warnings, not test failures.

Fixed all three findings.

- TS/TSX type-import masking now ignores runtime `typeof import(resolve())` expressions and preserves multiline offsets.
- SQL statement masks now ignore ordinary, escaped, and dollar-quoted literal contents.
- Multi-name `DROP` handling now covers types, materialized views, foreign tables, collations, and statistics.

Regression coverage was added in [TypeScript tests](/Users/thomi/Projects/entire-graph/internal/sem/parser_typescript_regression_test.go:96) and [PostgreSQL tests](/Users/thomi/Projects/entire-graph/internal/sem/parser_postgres_regression_test.go:388).

Verification passed:

- `mise run test:ci`
- `mise run vet`
- `mise run build`
- `git diff --check`

Three files are modified and uncommitted.
No. The fixes are only in the local working tree—uncommitted and unpushed.
Well then, you know what to do
I’ll use the GitHub publishing workflow to review the exact diff, commit the three fixes intentionally, and push the current branch. I’ll avoid creating a duplicate PR if this branch already has one.

Scope is clean: only the three parser/test files from this fix are modified. The branch already has draft PR #50, so I’ll add a focused follow-up commit and push it to that existing PR rather than opening another one.
